runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 4 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) on taxable income elasticities and labor-supply margins
Keane (2011) review of labor supply elasticities
Eissa and Liebman (1996) / EITC-related labor supply evidence on participation vs. hours responses
runs.reasoningSummary: My memory is that prime-age primary earners typically have modest intensive-margin substitution elasticities, often lower than secondary earners, with extensive-margin responses comparatively limited. For a bounded effective-marginal-wage response coefficient in decile 4, I place the center around 0.15, allowing a fairly wide upper tail for modeling choices and definition differences.
